abstract: Gratitude is a fundamental expression of faith for believers who have experienced divine grace. This study investigates the theological perception of thanksgiving as presented in 1 Corinthians 1:4-9, examining how Paul’s exhortations served to strengthen and evaluate the conduct of the early Christian community in Corinth. Paul’s gratitude was rooted in the conviction that salvation is an act of God alone, who sovereignly calls His people from spiritual death into new life. Utilizing a descriptive qualitative methodology, this research analyzes the text to demonstrate its enduring utility in assessing and encouraging the spiritual lives of both historical and contemporary believers. The findings reveal that the Corinthian Christians were anchored by the assurance of their calling and the sufficiency of the grace bestowed through Jesus Christ. Ultimately, this study concludes that Paul’s framework for thanksgiving remains deeply relevant, offering a timeless model for modern believers to recognize and respond to God’s grace in their daily lives.
